CORNISH: Yes. Overreach is real.

DUBKE: It is. And a temporary -- while this was a really temporary pause, it seems --

CORNISH: We can't tell. We can't tell.

DUBKE: But this is also where we're having the problems with ICE almost consistently. So, you've got to take a look at that and go, either we need more training -- I disagree that we need to stop it. I do think that it's an effective tool, but it's obviously not one that these ICE agents are utilizing effectively.

CORNISH: I want to play something for you on another topic. J.D. Vance was on Joe Rogan's podcast talking about many things we discussed today, but one of them was kind of continuing to soak the conversation around the L.A. mayor's race, which California famously has a delay in the count. And, in the end, the Trump-endorsed candidate, Spencer Pratt, did not win.

Here is how J.D. Vance characterized it all.

J.D. VANCE, VICE PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES: But it just so happened that the third place person, in relative terms, did a lot better than the first and second place person.

JOE ROGAN, HOST, "THE JOE ROGAN EXPERIENCE": Disproportionately better. VANCE: Such that the Republican was actually kicked out of the race so that there's not actually a real election in California. That, to me, was the crazy part. It's like you were designing the vote share in order to kick Pratt out and put the third person into second place. That, to me, is like very, very bizarre.

CORNISH: Having actually run an election in California, do you feel as though something was going on with the counts?

KILEY: No. I don't think anything was going on. That being said, I think that the fact that we count our votes so slow in California is a big problem and --

CORNISH: But does that help?

KILEY: No. I mean I think that we should focus on this as an issue of basic competence and public trust. And "The New York Times" editorial board has agreed with that, "The Washington Post" editorial board has agreed with that, that California needs to count votes faster. People should have the results on election night or close to it. When you drag it out for weeks, then it does create opportunities for people -- and it is kind of odd when just your person who doesn't follow things as closely as we might sees that one person's ahead on election day and another person's ahead weeks later. You know, it's just because the votes are taking a long time to count. There's not necessarily anything nefarious about it. But every other state manages to --

CORNISH: Will it help if the president brings it up in his speech?

KILEY: I don't think it necessarily helps. I think that those of us -- there's a broad consensus that California needs to do a better job of counting votes. And, you know, I think that it just should focus on that as an issue of basic administrative competence.

CORNISH: Lastly, we started the show talking about this vote on military humanitarian aid to Israel. What was your vote? And give us your reason.

KILEY: I voted against the amendment to cut off the aid. Maintaining the American-Israeli relationship, which is mutually beneficial, which is vital to our national security interests in America, has always been broadly bipartisan. It was bipartisan yesterday as well. Hakeem Jeffries, the Democrat leader, opposed that amendment, too. So, I was glad to see that it failed overwhelmingly. Though it is concerning that you had 100 some members who voted for it.

CORNISH: We heard people like AOC. We also heard Jasmine Crockett make an argument that actually was turned towards the economy, turned towards the idea that the U.S. should not be spending this kind of money abroad.

And we talked about this at the start. And I remember Republicans said this kind of thing about Ukraine, right? Like, there's always a conversation in American politics about what it -- what foreign aid really means.

So, to you, what do you say to those voters who are questioning U.S. foreign aid to Israel and its war operations?

KILEY: Well, of course, every dollar needs to be heavily scrutinized. But if you look at the cost, if Israel were to lose its qualitative military advantage over Iran and its terrorist proxies in the region, these are groups, Hezbollah, Hamas, who do not like the United States and could pose a national security risk to the United States. And so having a strong alliance with the one democratic country in the region, having mutually beneficial technology sharing agreements, intelligence sharing, that is absolutely in the national security interest in the United States and the cost, economic and otherwise, if we were to lose that, would be absolutely immense.

CORNISH: So, when you see the Gallup polling showing those shifts across the American public in their support for this particular Israeli government, do you think, gee, I've got more convincing to do to convince all these Americans that actually this is important or is there something else going on?

KILEY: Well, support for this particular Israeli government is a different question. Of course, you know, people can have different opinions about the Netanyahu government and others that have been at the helm in Israel, just as we'll have different opinions of our own government here in the United States from administration to administration. But the American-Israeli alliance is something that has been enduring and has been bipartisan. And so --

CORNISH: But one affects the other.

KILEY: But certainly, one affects the other. But I think that we need to understand that this is something that is larger than any one administration in either country when it comes to America's national interests.

CORNISH: OK, Congressman, stick around. This is Congressman Kevin Kiley of California.

Next on CNN, Georgia's 2020 election results could be part of the president's address tonight. Former lieutenant Governor Geoff Duncan joins us.

Plus, there is a search for a leaker inside the West Wing. How far officials are going to go to find the source.

CORNISH: OK, so President Trump's primetime address tonight likely aiming to put the 2020 election back in focus. And that means putting Georgia back in the spotlight. Several investigations found the election was free and fair. The president still doesn't buy it. Six years later, people he backs for important roles are still backing the false narrative of a stolen election.

SEN. JON OSSOFF (D-GA): Who won the 2020 election?

JAY CLAYTON, DNI NOMINEE: You know, we're not -- I'm not -- I'm not going to do this with you.

OSSOFF: Who won the 2020 election?

CLAYTON: I think I've answered the question. We can keep doing this.

OSSOFF: You refuse to answer a basic question about who won a presidential election --

OSSOFF: But you asked to lead America's intelligence community? Isn't it humiliating to be unable to answer this question, to have to indulge the president's delusions?

CORNISH: Joining me now, Georgia's now former Republican lieutenant governor, who held office during that election, Geoff Duncan.

So, needless to say, you have had this conversation with Trump officials in the past. What did you make of how Blanche answered?

GEOFF DUNCAN FORMER GEORGIA LIEUTENANT GOVERNOR: Well, good for Jon Ossoff and the others that held him accountable in the public square.

I mean, look, this is -- this is so hard to believe that not only do people of this stature actually follow these narratives and try to push these conspiracy theories, but the others, the masses, follow them and believe them, either for political expediency or just because they just don't take the time to actually pay attention to the details.

I think what's going to be frustrating tonight is that if history predicts the future, then this speech is going to be an hour long worth of just conspiracy theories and a mile wide and an inch deep with no facts. And it's going to be talking about solutions in search of a problem over and over and over and over again.

CORNISH: I want to ask you about this. Jon Ossoff obviously leads the Senate race in Georgia right now according to a Fox News poll, 56 to 43. Now, Manu Raju of CNN was asking his Republican opponent, Mike Collins, a similar question, and here's how Collins answered.

REP. MIKE COLLINS (R), GEORGIA SENATE CANDIDATE: And that's what happened in 2020. They changed the rules up. You don't all of a sudden turn around and start mailing out absentee ballots to everybody. You don't set up drop boxes all over the place for people to just start this mass harvesting, ballot harvesting.

MANU RAJU, CNN CHIEF CONGRESSIONAL CORRESPONDENT: Do you think Trump won?

COLLINS: Trump won that race.

CORNISH: Georgia is an interesting place because people like you ended up kind of turning against Trump and the Republican Party itself. It's the state that gives us Warnock, but also Marjorie Taylor Greene. At this point, what is the sort of political fallout in Georgia from the persistent dialog about 2020?

DUNCAN: Well, it's answers like that from Mike Collins and the job that Jon Ossoff has done, which is the reason why Jon Ossoff is going to win this race. I think Georgians are smart enough to be able to pay attention to the actual person. I mean, I think that's why you saw, in previous election cycles, Raphael Warnock win and Brian Kemp win. One, obviously, a Democrat. One, obviously, a Republican. And so, I think Georgians are able to pay attention.

And we've seen this play out in three dimensions. We've watched Donald Trump come in and literally rip the soul out of a state just because he was too prideful to admit he got whipped. He got whipped because he was in a terrible place with the voters. He wanted to double down on nonsense. And then he caused two U.S. senators to lose because he put a ring in their nose and dragged them around with a chain for literally weeks in a runoff, trying to make them agree with the fact that the election was rigged.

It wasn't rigged. We all know that. Were there things that we can do to improve? We should all be open-minded enough to know that this is an iterative process with elections. We should make improvements as time goes on. But there was nothing that was rigged in that election we (ph) know.

CORNISH: And we expect to -- yes, we expect to hear the president also talk about the SAVE Act, which is something he's pushing for, which would involve new I.D. requirements for states.

So, I'm sure we're going to hear a lot more. Geoff Duncan, thank you so much.

Very quickly, Mike, I wanted to get your opinion, just because him saying like a ring in the nose, you know what I mean? Like, he -- this is a good example of how irritated some Republicans were in Georgia after the pressure to overturn the election. But him bringing in the fact that he thinks this affects, like Trump going into different races and trying to make a difference, he doesn't see it as a good thing. DUBKE: I think what we saw yesterday in the Clayton testimony with the

Democrats, one after the other after the other, asking the same question, that was -- that was performance art by the Democrats, asking somebody who has been nominated for a job to say something that his -- the new boss doesn't believe.

Whether or not you agree with Donald Trump. Donald Trump does believe he won in 2020. And this -- I am so tired of this question being asked because I do think it is performative art by the -- by the Democrats and the fact that Republicans keep bringing it up.

CORNISH: OK, Meghan, jump in.

DUBKE: I'm sorry, I just --

CORNISH: I wanted to get your point of --

DUBKE: It drives me nuts. They don't get to real issues because they keep wanting to ask this question.

HAYS: But here's the thing. But if they -- it's an integrity --

CORNISH: They don't want to say two and two is five.

HAYS: It's an integrity question and it's who you stand for. and you're going to debate someone, Jay Clayton, who is very well respected, is going to debase himself knowing Joe Biden won that election --

DUBKE: Clayton yesterday said that Joe Biden won the electoral votes.

HAYS: Then why couldn't he just say yes to Jon Ossoff?

DUBKE: He did. He did say that.

HAYS: When Jon Ossoff asked, why didn't he say yes? Because that's core stuff.

DUBKE: Because he's not going to play the game with the Democrats. He --

HAYS: And he -- now he humiliated himself.

CORNISH: All right, let's let a lawmaker jump in. Is it a game, is it a gotcha to ask someone whether or not President Trump lost the election, or to simply who won the election? Is that a gotcha moment?

KYLIE: I mean, it shouldn't be a hard question. It's clearly there's an answer.

KYLIE: President Biden won. I, you know, I don't know why we continue to dwell on this on either side.

CORNISH: It's performance, says Mike.

DUBKE: It is performance.

CORNISH: It's performance and answer as well as question.

HAYS: Well, because -- because he -- but the problem is that Donald Trump keeps bringing it up. He's doing an entire primetime address on it tonight talking about election security and election fraud and how the 2020 election was stolen.

And just a few days ago, saying that the senators from Georgia are illegitimate. What does that say to the voters in Georgia? I mean you're -- this is -- he is continuing to make this an issue.

HAYS: If he just let it go and lived his life as the 47th president, we would be in a much better place.

DUBKE: Meghan, I do not disagree with you on that point.

But I will say, being in the White House in 2017 and having to deal with all of the Democrats saying that Donald Trump wasn't -- wasn't properly elected because he didn't win the popular vote in 2017, even though he won the electoral vote, which is the way we play this game in America --

DUBKE: That was -- we dealt with that for months, and it got played over and over and over again. So, this is that, in my mind, the same performative art again. But now it's --

CORNISH: So, you're looking forward to this speech, you're like --

HAYS: But you don't have Hillary Clinton running around being like, I was the president.

DUBKE: You know what --

HAYS: Like, I mean, this is just insane.

HAYS: No, it's worth asking just because, in general, like the more you talk about it, the more it remains an issue. And this week we're in an inflection point with Iran. This week we are talking about rising prices. There are definitely five other topics I'd like to hear out of a primetime address that we will not. DUBKE: Yes.

BOBIC: It's a gotcha question, but it's an effective one, I think, for Democrats --

CORNISH: Yes, Ossoff looked happy about --

BOBIC: Because what are we talking about?

BOBIC: What are we talking about right now?

DUBKE: We are -- well, not the Reflecting Pool.

DUBKE: Because they're --

DUBKE: I guess there's something good coming out of it.

CORNISH: It's OK. I can bring you some green water if you want to relive it.

All right, we're going to talk about what is in your group chat. We have a guest. It is the congressman.

What is in your group chat?

KILEY: Well, my wife and I are expecting our first child. So, we're excited about that.

CORNISH: Your first child.

CORNISH: Oh my gosh. Yes, I'm excited.

KILEY: So, a little girl and (INAUDIBLE).

CORNISH: Oh, you even know the gender and everything. Oh, wow.

KILEY: So, no, very exciting. Hitting all the milestones there.

CORNISH: Shouldn't you be somewhere buying her something? A special pillow?

HAYS: She's very early.

CORNISH: Yes. So, we don't have to check your testosterone levels, is what you're saying.

CORNISH: That's not -- that's not in the text. It doesn't -- high t Kylie because it's --

KYLIE: Ask the secretary of war.

CORNISH: OK. Igor, what about you?

BOBIC: Sorry in advance, but --

CORNISH: What could be worse than what I just said? Go on.

BOBIC: This horrible stomach bug that's going around across the country.

CORNISH: Oh, OK, you win.

BOBIC: But I'll say this, I'm loving it because --

CORNISH: Because you don't have it.

BOBIC: You're giving me an excuse to eat, you know, the terrible food. Just, you know, no vegetables. Great. Awesome.

CORNISH: Yes, so, under you we're going to just put a banner that says, man won't eat salad anyway.

CORNISH: Just so we're clear.

HAYS: I've been saying, fast food's the way to go. I don't know why people fight me on it. I don't get sick.

DUBKE: Well, that's where they got it, though. It was a fast food joint where they put lettuce under some meat.

CORNISH: We don't I it was lettuce for sure.

HAYS: We don't -- we don't know that. How would you know?

DUBKE: Or there is cilantro, actually.

CORNISH: They are still looking it up. I don't know. Listen, the folks at Taco Bell have subtracted many ingredients because they don't have information.

DUBKE: I'm hearing cilantro.

HAYS: And we don't have the people in the CDC to look at it, so we have no idea.

CORNISH: Oh my God, this group is awesome.

CORNISH: Just so everyone knows, these two are now officially in my phone on a group chat.

CORNISH: And it says Thursday heated rivalry.

CORNISH: That's the name of it in my phone.

DUBKE: My -- seriously, in my group chat, there was a picture of Taco Bell gain of function lab that had this particular topic.

CORNISH: I don't know. I --

DUBKE: But that's not my group chat.

CORNISH: That's -- what is your group chat, Mike?

DUBKE: I'm just telling you that's in another group chat I --

CORNISH: I like it. I like it.

HAYS: What's your group chat?

DUBKE: My group chat is lock the clock. I appreciate what the House did trying to get --

CORNISH: This is the Daylight Saving --

DUBKE: But they did it wrong.

CORNISH: Did you vote for this?

KYLIE: I did. And, you know, there's a debate, do you do standard? Do you do Daylight?

DUBKE: They get it wrong.

KYLIE: I think both are better than going back and forth.

DUBKE: Standard is better than daylight. It cuts down on heart attacks, strokes, a lot -- better -- better sleep.

DUBKE: It's safer for children.

CORNISH: I'm pro-standard.

DUBKE: We go standard.

CORNISH: But that's not where we are.

DUBKE: It was a bunch of retailers that pushed for them to go Daylight.

HAYS: No. Of course we don't do it the way we should do it. We did the opposite of what we should do.

CORNISH: What's going on there?

KYLIE: Well, look, we can always revisit it. I think that the --

HAYS: The last time this happened --

CORNISH: When you have --

KYLIE: Not going back and forth anymore is better than -- you know, it's better than that.

CORNISH: When you have a newborn, that's when we're going to revisit it, this spring forward, OK?

CORNISH: Last word to you, Meghan.

HAYS: I spent the weekend in -- a long weekend in Vegas. And the argument over humidity, the heat versus a dry heat. And I am team dry heat all the way.

CORNISH: Dry heat all the way. All the way.

DUBKE: Who is on team humidity?

HAYS: I don't know. All the people who live on the East Coast that never go west.

CORNISH: We don't like the humidity. That's what we always say, it's not the heat it's the humidity.

DUBKE: Oh, my goodness.

HAYS: West Coast. West Coast.

CORNISH: Oh, my God, group chat.

HAYS: The Congressman and I can I agree on that. KYLIE: There you have it (ph).

CORNISH: Thank you so much for waking up with us. You could be anywhere, but somehow you're here with us. The headlines are next.

100+ Democrats Vote Against Sending Aid to Israel; Gallup Poll: U.S. Opinion of Israel Drops 29 Points in 5 Years; Looksmaxxing the Military: Testosterone Testing for Troops; Todd Blanche Under Fire in hearings. Aired 6-6:30a ET

Aired July 16, 2026 - 06:00   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: OK. Good morning, everybody. We're going to be talking about whether Israel's right-wing government is losing America. New criticism from the vice president and a stunning new vote by House Democrats.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: So, this is an important moment, because nothing will be the same on this issue ever again, I think, after this vote.

CORNISH: And President Trump promising, quote, "really big news" in tonight's primetime address. Will it actually be old gripes about the 2020 election?

PETE HEGSETH, U.S. DEFENSE SECRETARY: I'm authorizing a new screening program for testosterone deficiency for our service members.

CORNISH: So, the Pentagon plans to test the troops for low T. Is this really a problem in the ranks?

J.D. VANCE, U.S. VICE PRESIDENT: I say this with all candor. Like, we absolutely screwed up the comms of the Epstein files.

CORNISH: A big admission from J.D. Vance. The question is, who could now take the fall for it?

REP. ALEXANDRIA OCASIO-CORTEZ (D-NY): We're not going to do it. The American people don't want to stand for this anymore.

CORNISH: OK. More evidence of a massive shift in Congress. More than 100 Democrats vote against sending aid to Israel. So, what does the vote mean for the midterms and beyond?

Good morning, everybody. I'm Audie Cornish, and this is where we start: this movement in the House to end U.S. military aid to Israel. It's drawing more support than it ever has.

So, 103 Democrats voted in favor of the amendment, which was sponsored by Kentucky Republican Thomas Massie. Ultimately, the amendment failed. The Republican majority and 98 Democrats voted it down.

For progressive Democrats, the vote signals a shift they once never thought was possible.

REP. DELIA RAMIREZ (D-IL): I believe, looking at this list, that we're going to pick up more members, people who are saying that we cannot use taxpayer dollars for endless wars.

OCASIO-CORTEZ: To me, what it says is that the majority of the Democratic Party is -- is most in touch with the American electorate, writ large.

REP. ILHAN OMAR (D-MN): There are many members who took votes that I was surprised to take that yes vote today. And I am proud of them.

CORNISH: Joining me now in the group chat, Harrison Mann, former U.S. Army major and former executive officer of the Defense Intelligence Agency's Middle East and Africa center; Mike Dubke, a former communications director at the Trump White House; and Meghan Hays, former director of message planning at the Biden White House.

So, first, I just want to start with -- I always joke that Congress is a lagging indicator. They're usually not at the forefront of things.

So, here's Gallup poll in February showing the overall opinion of Israel. And the difference is from 2021 to now. And you can see the net favorables are down 29 percent. It's a massive shift in a very short amount of time.

Harrison, I want to start with you because full disclosure to the audience, you obviously left your job in government over Israel's actions in Gaza. So, this is very close to you. We know your opinion. What do you think of this moment and this shift in Congress?

HARRISON MANN, FORMER EXECUTIVE OFFICER, DEFENSE INTELLIGENCE AGENCY: I think on one hand, it's a massive victory for anti-war, pro-peace Americans who have been pushing for this for years, who want to reevaluate the U.S. relationship with Israel.

And, you know, on the other hand, we have to understand why we suddenly have up to two dozen new Democrats finally voting to -- to withhold aid to Israel.

And it's not just because they suddenly found religion or changed their mind about their sense of morality. It means they are finally more afraid of their own voters than they are of pro-Israel donors and PACs like AIPAC. That is the shift.

The American people at large, and specifically Democratic voters, have wanted to see this for a long time. You know, recent polling shows, I think about 15 percent, only 15 percent of Democratic voters are supportive of Israel. That's a fringe position. That's the same number of Democratic voters who want to ban abortion.

And so, we are seeing gradually elected Democrats catch up to where the voters are. But on the other hand, this is still quite an undemocratic outcome when you have half of Democrats in the House voting against the will of their voters.

So, to your point on public on polling, I looked at an A.P. poll. This was in June. Republicans' view on U.S. support for Israel. It's the 2024 versus now.

And we're seeing some shift there, as well. It's a little more of a divide, obviously, than Democrats, but we're seeing a shift.

Then you have people like J.D. Vance. He was just -- he's on his book tour. He's still talking. And he was --

MIKE DUBKE, FORMER TRUMP WHITE HOUSE COMMUNICATIONS DIRECTOR: Still on the book tour?

CORNISH: He's still talking.

MEGHAN HAYS, FORMER BIDEN WHITE HOUSE DIRECTOR OF MESSAGE PLANNING: Keep on talking. Still talking.

CORNISH: I mean, well, he's no longer on a ceasefire deal, OK? Because that's toast.

So, he's still talking. But on that deal, he was saying that it was the Israeli government that was sort of undermining the atmosphere around this deal. Here he is.

VANCE: There's a lot of talk about how -- how much is the Israeli government influencing American politics? And there are certainly certain people within the Israeli government who hate the deal. And we see exact evidence.

There's a lot of talk about how -- how much is the Israeli government influencing American politics? And there are certainly certain people within the Israeli government who hate the deal. And we see exact evidence.

CORNISH: So, he's not saying, "Joe Rogan settle down. Joe Rogan, we -- our relationship with Israel" -- you know what I mean? He's saying, Look, I understand. I hear you. What do you make of how he's trying to navigate the politics of this?

CORNISH: Like he's at the heart of it. He actually --

DUBKE: He's at the heart of it. But I do think that there's two separate -- two separate thoughts here, one of which is I'm not -- I'm not sure that this -- what the vote yesterday that we saw, to me, was less about America and more about this ongoing civil war that I think is emerging within the Democratic Party.

CORNISH: Despite the Republican conversation, as well?

CORNISH: Tucker leaving the party?

DUBKE: Well, I think -- look, I think on the Republican side, a lot of this revolves around just the morality of foreign aid, whether or not we should be giving U.S. taxpayer dollars to any country.

DUBKE: Let alone -- or let alone --

DUBKE: -- holding Israel out.

CORNISH: So, then let me play for you Jasmine Crockett, because I don't think you usually agree, but here's what she had to say after the vote.

REP. JASMINE CROCKETT (D-TX): I don't want people who are already hurting, especially as we're heading into these significant midterms, to look at the Democrats and say, you know what? We can't afford gas. We can't afford food. Yet, you guys are still sending money elsewhere.

DUBKE: And that's a very Republican position. I'm surprised she didn't win her primary.

HAYS: So, I actually think that is more about what this is than necessarily the Israel-Gaza conflict. I think a lot of the voters and constituents in their districts are saying, We can't afford our own groceries and gas, but we're spending a significant amount of aid to foreign countries, and our debt is increasing. But I also don't want to overcrank on this. In swing districts, this

is not even a top five issue for voters. So, we are not seeing this movement as we are seeing online. This might be an issue in New York City or in some of these very blue districts.

CORNISH: Or in 100 districts, right?

HAYS: But that's -- that's my -- like, this is not something that I think is really impacting the Democratic Party. And I do think this is a fair conversation that we need to be having as a country. And Congress needs to have. How much foreign aid are we going to be sending places?

I don't think this is special to Israel. I think it is in general, when we have such a crisis here with affordability, that we are spending so much of our taxpayer dollars abroad. And I think that's what this about.

CORNISH: Well, let me add one more thing.

CORNISH: Because Rahm Emanuel went to Israel. He's trying to obviously lay down some -- some stones on the road to 2028. And here is what he had to say.

RAHM EMMANUEL, FORMER OBAMA WHITE HOUSE CHIEF OF STAFF: So, I told the Israeli public, this is the price. You have lost America. It's not a Democratic Party problem.

When two-thirds of the American people are not supporting Israel. And for ages 30 and younger, it's down to 21. That's not a Democratic Party problem. That's an America problem. And I thought it's better to be honest, better to be direct.

DUBKE: It's crazy to me that every Democrat running for president is going to Israel. First we had Ro Khanna there this week, basically trying to --

CORNISH: I think that's two. It's Rahm and Ro.

DUBKE: Well, I think more of them will go.

DUBKE: I do. I -- I think that they are trying to create this divide. And this is where I come back to the civil war within the Democratic Party.

I think we're seeing this issue really take root within the Democratic Party debate. I think on the Republican side is exactly what Meghan saying, that it's foreign aid or not foreign aid. This is specifically about Israel with the Democrats.

CORNISH: What do you see?

MANN: Well, first, for the Republicans, I'm not sure it's about foreign aid, because every single Republican voted against this bill to restrict aid to Israel, except for one, Thomas Massie, who Trump has chased out of office.

And within the Democrats, it absolutely is a civil war. But again, within elected leaders, because the voters are solidly on one side of this.

And I do think there is something specific to Israel because of the horrific nature of the crimes in Gaza, voters understand that this this is an accurate litmus test for whether or not a politician shares their morality and whether or not a politician is beholden to their donors over their voters. And that's why this is a special issue.

HAYS: I don't necessarily agree with that in the Democratic Party, because I think that there are a faction of Democrats that think that. And then there are the Democrats who are middle-class working people who are like, why are we spending money and can't afford things?

So,, I don't necessarily think this issue is 100 percent morality for every single Democrat. And that's not where the party stands writ large.

And you can see that in some of these races, that they are not voting for people based on their stance on Israel or Gaza, or whether they called it a genocide. They're voting based on whether they can afford groceries and gas, and that is where they're coming down on this.

So, I just think that there's a split and that that is, to your point, a civil war in the Democratic Party.

CORNISH: OK. Coming up on CNN THIS MORNING, we're going to talk to a congressman about his vote on aid to Israel. Representative Kevin Kiley. He is an independent, and he joins us in the next half hour.

Plus, top Trump officials confiscating phones as the hunt for White House leakers reaches a new level.

And then the Pentagon, they want to test the troops' testosterone. Is Hegseth looksmaxxing the military?

HEGSETH: I'm authorizing a new screening program for testosterone deficiency for our service members, ensuring you have the right testosterone levels to operate at your absolute best. (END VIDEO CLIP)

CORNISH: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth wants our troops to be tested for testosterone deficiency. He says it will make them stronger.

So, here's how the plan will work. Anyone 30 years and older will be required to test. Those under 30, they can volunteer. And anyone diagnosed with a deficiency can voluntarily choose to receive treatment.

The Trump administration has been pushing for easier access to these therapies. In December, the FDA hosted an expert panel focusing on the benefits of testosterone replacement therapy for members of the military.

And there are concerns.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: Testosterone replacement therapy can actually affect deployability and eligibility for special duty status. There are service members who work their entire careers to get these special duty statuses that are very highly sought after, and they worry that, if they get seen for this disease and treated for it, that they may lose that status.

CORNISH: The group chat is back. Just to help clarify that point for people: testosterone at a certain level is an anabolic steroid, and in world sports competitions, it is considered a performance-enhancing drug.

So, that's why he's talking about people getting in trouble for it in the past.

Now, since it was me and Meghan and Mike, we had to bring in more testosterone. That's why Harrison is here. And right now, I was looking at a tweet. Chrissy Houlahan saying online, "I'm sure the testosterone industry is thrilled. Taxpayers should ask, who benefits from this latest culture war obsession?"

Is this something people might be excited about? Young men in the military?

MANN: Yes, they might be, but they need to consult their -- their doctor. Like, we've got to first be clear: Pete Hegseth is not somebody who -- who understands or cares about the health of the troops under his responsibility.

CORNISH: Why do you say that? He's always talking about their weight.

MANN: Yes. Let's -- let's --

CORNISH: Always talking about exercising.

CORNISH: Facial hair.

MANN: Yes. I've got to remind the viewers his last big medical announcement was canceling mandatory flu shots, a policy he had to reverse when a bunch of people at Air Force basic training got sick, and one died.

So, I don't trust him for medical advice.

Is it good to offer everybody in the military gender-affirming care who wants it? Absolutely. I don't think that privilege should only be reserved for Pete Hegseth's conception of his ideal man, and that's what he's doing right now.

CORNISH: I do think, though you've now hit on the culture war nerve center when you said "gender-affirming care."

CORNISH: This is what a lot of people are saying here. Senator Tammy Duckworth saying, "It sounds like gender-affirming care to me."

And of course, like, I'm not holding my breath for his perimenopause screening, but it's obviously --

HAYS: Yes, they don't promote women --

HAYS: -- so they don't -- doesn't matter.

CORNISH: Obviously, this is an issue that's at the next -- I think it's at the junction of, like, Chad Looksmaxxing Boulevard and RFK Way. Like, there's a world that is obsessed with testosterone, because they're also obsessed with a couple other things that have to do with population decline or these other issues affecting men.

DUBKE: There is one thing that Pete Hegseth and this administration is focused on, and that is recruiting more young men.

DUBKE: Especially. But young people to the military.

DUBKE: We went through years of having our -- missing our military recruitment numbers.

CORNISH: Yes. I want to show that.

DUBKE: And while this may seem --

CORNISH: The military news has been reporting about the best numbers in 15 years. DUBKE: Yes.

CORNISH: Show that while you're talking.

DUBKE: And -- and I think this is part of it.

MANN: That has to do with the economy.

DUBKE: I think this is part of it, that we are -- that -- that they are focused on this. And this may seem bizarre on its face, that video that you showed. But it is part of this recruitment of young men joining the military.

CORNISH: I don't think it's bizarre. Have you seen the commercials for testosterone? Like, this is a massive industry.

DUBKE: Yes, Doug Flutie. Doug Flutie has a great one. That's -- but this is my point.

CORNISH: But can I get -- Harrison just said something here under his breath. You don't think it is vibes. You think it's something else that's drawing people?

MANN: Yes. I mean, it's not -- it's not vibes. It's part of -- like, this -- this video is --

CORNISH: This is what will happen to you, by the way, if you take it. Yes.

MANN: Yes. Like the video's goofy. Pete Hegseth posting we're going to have a high T military, I guess implying that up until now it was -- it was low T is goofy.

But the vision is not silly. And that's basically a white male military in Pete Hegseth's image. Like that's -- that's why he thinks about this stuff.

DUBKE: I think -- now I think you're -- Now you're going overboard with this.

MANN: He's -- he is firing or killing the careers of every black or female senior officer he can get his hands on. And we can't understand the other culture war stuff outside of that context.

He has his vision of, like, sweaty masculinity, kind of Pete Hegseth type of guy. He is trying to make the military look like that.

I don't think he'll be successful. But these, you know, seemingly kind of incongruous announcements for a man who's supposed to be managing a massive war right now are -- are a little more frightening than they seem.

HAYS: But also, we are now living in a place where we have canceled or we have canceled funding for HHS for -- HHS for a lot of different medical research and for science.

And we now have a massive outbreak that people are getting from their food, because we don't have people monitoring food anymore.

Where is the scientific research that has said this -- that this -- this could actually cause infertility in men. This can cause a lot of damage to men. Where is the research, and where is the warning to the men?

CORNISH: Well, to be fair, I do want to offer some facts. There's a man named Dr. Hagai Levine in Israel. They have done massive studies and meta-analysis, and male sperm counts and testosterone, it's down.

What is not in agreement within the scientific community is how to deal with it, or what is causing it. Is it obesity? Is it diabetes? Is it sitting around in the pandemic? Right? There's a number of things.

HAYS: A hundred percent.

CORNISH: So, it's not even clear that giving TRT on the government dime to a bunch -- a male population is the absolute solve.

HAYS: And then what happens when it does do something --

CORNISH: And whether the military should be solving this issue for us, societally.

DUBKE: I'm not saying they're trying to solve the issue.

CORNISH: I know. I'm talking to Meghan.

DUBKE: I don't know. You're looking at me and talking to her.

CORNISH: We're friends. It's a group chat.

HAYS: I just -- I just think that there's a lot here that is underlying current of what we're trying to do to our military, and who we're trying to isolate from the military, and who we're trying to recruit to the military.

But one thing that you said that I actually think is important about the recruitment is we are in a terrible economy. People are joining the military, because it gives them opportunities. It gives them a place to live. It gives them food. It gives them money when they sign up.

So, I think that there's a lot of ways out for people who don't necessarily think that college or there's other jobs for them. So, I think that's another reason that recruitment is up.

DUBKE: Recruitment was down when we had an even worse economy. So, I'm not buying that. I --

CORNISH: It was down, yes. HAYS: Totally. But that also could be a reason. Getting -- getting your free, like, T shot from the military isn't going to drive people.

DUBKE: But here's the weird part, and this is why I'm saying it's bizarre. This is basically for those officers who are over 30 years of age, who have been in the military for ten, 12, 15 years.

But what it attracts -- and the point I'm trying to make is -- it attracts those young 16-, 17-, 18-year-olds who are buying into this cultural conversation of high testosterone.

HAYS: And this -- herein lies the problem.

HAYS: Herein lies the problem of how we are -- how people are being recruited, and how people are being influenced --

HAYS: -- and what they want to make our military.

DUBKE: And we can have that debate. But I think this is a recruitment tool.

CORNISH: You can -- Yes, I could be testing T levels to come on the panel, too. I've been thinking about instituting that.

DUBKE: I am raged about this right now.

CORNISH: I just think that is only fair.

Harrison Mann, thank you so much for being here. Really appreciate your time and expertise and point of view.

After the break on CNN THIS MORNING, President Trump could return to a familiar playbook. Tonight, he's going to be talking about the 2020 election.

And Vice President Vance admits the administration messed up on the Epstein files.

VANCE: I say this with all candor, like, we absolutely screwed up the comms of the Epstein files. Like, we just did. But do I think the reason we screwed up the comms is because we were trying to hide something? No.

CORNISH: Vice President J.D. Vance admitting the administration screwed up on the Epstein files. This comes as the president's pick for attorney general takes the stand in his confirmation hearing, having to own up to that process.

TODD BLANCHE, ACTING U.S. ATTORNEY GENERAL: Any mistake that was made is not appropriate. And so, yes, I am sorry that, in about 1 percent of the -- of the documents, mistakes were made.

But what I will say on top of that is we put tons of resources to rectifying those mistakes immediately.

CORNISH: Joining us in the group chat, Igor Bobic, congressional reporter at "NOTUS."

Igor, can I start with you? We are starting here with the Epstein issue. Is that the issue that took over this set of hearings, or did something else kind of move to the forefront?

IGOR BOBIC, CONGRESSIONAL REPORTER, "NOTUS": It was one of them. I believe that Todd Blanche had to put that out there to sort of appease some of these Republican senators who believed that the administration ought to have done more about this Epstein issue.

But really, the overarching kind of thing that Republican senators wanted to hear is about this. You know, John Cornyn and Thom Tillis really wanted to get more information out of Blanche about this weaponization fund, anti-weaponization fund.

CORNISH: Let me play that exchange for people. So, this is related to the settlement that Trump and his family made with the IRS over -- they believe that the IRS was weaponized against them.

And the settlement led to a potential fund for reparations, reparations for people who felt they were wronged from the government.

And Texas Senator John Cornyn, who lost his primary, is the person who raised this very pointed question.

SEN. JOHN CORNYN (R-TX): First of all, I believe you have said that the weaponization fund is a moot issue. Is that your position?

BLANCHE: Yes, it is a moot issue, meaning there is no weaponization fund. The weaponization fund is dead. It's not moving forward.

CORNISH: Did it feel like that was enough for Cornyn?

Return to Transcripts main page

Rep. Kevin Kiley (I-CA) is Interviewed about ICE Stops; Former Georgia Lieutenant Governor Geoff Duncan is Interviewed about Elections; Trump-Backed DNI Nominee Dodges 2020 Question. Aired 6:30- 7a ET

Aired July 16, 2026 - 06:30   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

TODD BLANCHE, ACTING ATTORNEY GENERAL: It is a moot issue, meaning there is no weaponization fund. The weaponization fund is dead. It's not moving forward.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: Did it feel like that was enough for Cornyn? It seemed -- oh, both shaking your head. What's going on there?

MIKE DUBKE, FORMER TRUMP WHTIE HOUSE COMMUNICATIONS DIRECTOR: Well, he basically said it wasn't enough.

CORNISH: But I always watch which senators seem like a pretty sure vote, all of a sudden asking very pointed questions.

DUBKE: He, in the past, should have been a sure vote. But yes, when you get the rug pulled out from under you by an administration and now you're voting on, you know, that administration's nominees, you question this. This is part of the -- kind of the long-term gain, short term pain that I've talked about, that the Trump administration has created for itself by running primary opponents in Texas and Louisiana and elsewhere.

But Tillis also had similar questions and had a very different response while asking those questions.

DUBKE: And so, I can look at Tillis and go, OK, that's a yes vote. Cornyn still seems to be up in the air. And this is -- you know, this is drawing a lot of questions.

Now, they also have a legislative pathway forward that I think Cornyn may be trying to force, and that might be part of the larger story here.

CORNISH: OK. In the meantime, he was also asked -- this came up in very -- in various ways, are you the nation's top law enforcement officer working for the public, or are you Trump's personal attorney?

Here's one person asking that question, Senator Kennedy, I believe.

SEN. JOHN KENNEDY (R-LA): Are you and President Trump friends?

TODD BLANCHE, ACTING ATTORNEY GENERAL: I'm his lawyer. Was his lawyer. And now I'm the deputy attorney general.

CORNISH: Everyone kept sort of, oh, Freudian slip with something like that. But do you think that argument is catching, Meghan?

MEGHAN HAYS, FORMER BIDEN WHITE HOUSE DIRECTOR OF MESSAGE PLANNING: Yes, I think that everyone understands that the Justice Department works for Donald Trump and not for the American people. I think that's been made very clear in the actions that they have said. When you're at war with the judicial system, when the 16,000 people have quit the DOJ or been fired, when judges no longer can have, you know, trust in DOJ lawyers because there's been so many misleading and lies presented in cases, that's a real problem. That's not saying you're the lawyer for the American people. That's saying you're a lawyer for Donald Trump.

CORNISH: At the end of the day, did he perform better or worse than a Pam Bondi? We can -- we don't need to guess about whether he's going to get the job, but are we seeing a real fundamental shift in leadership at the Justice Department?

IGOR BOBIC, CONGRESSIONAL REPORTER, "NOTUS": Pam Bondi took a far more aggressive posture. She was attacking Democrats outright. He got into little tiffs with a senator of Democratic --

CORNISH: Yes, but no yelling about the Dow. No nothing.

BOBIC: But nothing -- no. No.

And Republicans I spoke to yesterday on The Hill said they appreciated that. They liked his tenure. He kind of brought down the temperature. I think he will get confirmed. But, you know, John Cornyn is a wild card here.

CORNISH: Oh, interesting. OK. Straight --

DUBKE: He was -- he was low t.

CORNISH: Which is -- which is a real thing in the Texas race, right?

CORNISH: That's what they're saying about Talarico, low t Talarico.

CORNISH: Oh, this show.

Straight ahead on CNN THIS MORNING, people in Texas, as we said once again, but this time we're talking about the weather. There are rising waters there. The state is still under a flood emergency. People are worried about the rain on the way.

Plus, it's been a week of deadly ICE encounters. Is Congress ever going to step in with oversight? We've got California Congressman Kevin Kiley with us live.

And good morning to Philadelphia as we take a live look at the Philly skyline.

CORNISH: All right, today there is going to be a public viewing set to be held for the 52-year-old Houston man who was killed by ICE last week. He was shot and killed by agents who claimed that he tried to flee a traffic stop. Now an FBI affidavit alleges drugs were found in the van.

His death, followed by the killing of another man just days later in Maine, who was also at the center of an attempted ICE traffic stop. Now, that latest shooting happened Monday. On Tuesday. DHS agents were told to pause pulling people over. By Wednesday, President Trump had reversed that decision, calling traffic stops one of ICE's, quote, "most important and effective tools."

But some in own party disagree.

SEN. SUSAN COLLINS (R-ME): To have them routinely done, when we have two recent shootings, that raise very serious questions to me, calls for a halt in that approach for now.

SEN. LISA MURKOWSKI (R-AK): I think you're sending a very conflicting message there. And as we have seen with these recent incidents where we have now at least three fatalities, the American public is rightly concerned.

CORNISH: Joining us now in the group chat, Congressman Kevin Kiley. He is an independent from California.

Thanks to you for being here.

REP. KEVIN KILEY (I-CA): Of course. CORNISH: I know you have called for very specific reforms to ICE in the past. This is something you have talked about. But I want to play for you Tom Homan, who was asked about this pause on vehicle stops in particular. Here he is.

TOM HOMAN, BORDER CZAR: It's not a policy change. It's a temporary pause.

I think at the end of this review, which would be a short review, ICE will be right back to doing what they do.

CORNISH: Should this be a temporary pause or a permanent change?

KILEY: Well, I mean, I think the question -- the issue is that Congress has not asserted itself in the way that it should here to provide some stability here. This is precisely why I have advocated for reforms that I think that are actually bipartisan.

CORNISH: Do you think vehicle stops are valid?

KILEY: I think that the -- you know, the incidents we've seen have raised a lot of questions.

And so I think that this whiplash that we're saying we're going to pause, we're not going to have a pause, is precisely why we need to actually be forming policy here in a way that strikes the right balance, rather than just doing it sort of on a, you know, on an ad hoc basis.

When you have the sort of tragedies that we've seen, it's obviously created a great deal of distrust, which is why I advocated for the sort of common sense reforms to interior immigration enforcement that I think the vast majority of Americans support. Now --

CORNISH: Like what? What would you like to see? I know body cameras is a thing they're all fighting over.

KILEY: Yes, body cameras, training.

CORNISH: Is there anything else?

KILEY: Sure. Like I've voted in favor of requiring judicial warrants in order to enter homes, of having non-enforcement zones around schools and other sensitive areas, identification.

CORNISH: Is it easier now that you're an independent and not a Republican to say those things out loud?

KILEY: I don't know if it's easier. I mean, I've always been independent minded in the way that I -- and I made these, you know, similar arguments, regardless of my party affiliation.

But -- and I think that, you know, you had plenty of people on both sides who actually agreed that we do need reform like this. It's a very clear example of how the House has just -- Congress has fumbled an opportunity to do something good in a bipartisan way, which unfortunately has happened far too often.

CORNISH: Showing people right now, at least in California voters, do you approve of the job ICE is doing? Democrats, needless to say, disapprove, 94 percent. Independents, 67 percent. In the GOP, though, however, it's just 16 percent who disapprove.

And, Dubke, does this -- does each killing actually shift the mindset, or does it make the administration and those who agree with the mass deportation regime dig in deeper?

DUBKE: No, I do -- both. So, I do think it shifts the mindset of the American public when you've got a -- you know, Donald Trump ran on immigration and shutting down the border. He was incredibly effective in doing that after 2024. And we basically have a shutdown border at the moment.

Where he's failing, and I think where his -- where his numbers are now upside down in terms of immigration, are exactly because of these traffic stops.

CORNISH: Yes. Overreach is real.

DUBKE: It is. And a temporary -- while this was a really temporary pause, it seems --

CORNISH: We can't tell. We can't tell.

DUBKE: But this is also where we're having the problems with ICE almost consistently. So, you've got to take a look at that and go, either we need more training -- I disagree that we need to stop it. I do think that it's an effective tool, but it's obviously not one that these ICE agents are utilizing effectively.

CORNISH: I want to play something for you on another topic. J.D. Vance was on Joe Rogan's podcast talking about many things we discussed today, but one of them was kind of continuing to soak the conversation around the L.A. mayor's race, which California famously has a delay in the count. And, in the end, the Trump-endorsed candidate, Spencer Pratt, did not win.

Here is how J.D. Vance characterized it all.

J.D. VANCE, VICE PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES: But it just so happened that the third place person, in relative terms, did a lot better than the first and second place person.

JOE ROGAN, HOST, "THE JOE ROGAN EXPERIENCE": Disproportionately better. VANCE: Such that the Republican was actually kicked out of the race so that there's not actually a real election in California. That, to me, was the crazy part. It's like you were designing the vote share in order to kick Pratt out and put the third person into second place. That, to me, is like very, very bizarre.

CORNISH: Having actually run an election in California, do you feel as though something was going on with the counts?

KILEY: No. I don't think anything was going on. That being said, I think that the fact that we count our votes so slow in California is a big problem and --

CORNISH: But does that help?

KILEY: No. I mean I think that we should focus on this as an issue of basic competence and public trust. And "The New York Times" editorial board has agreed with that, "The Washington Post" editorial board has agreed with that, that California needs to count votes faster. People should have the results on election night or close to it. When you drag it out for weeks, then it does create opportunities for people -- and it is kind of odd when just your person who doesn't follow things as closely as we might sees that one person's ahead on election day and another person's ahead weeks later. You know, it's just because the votes are taking a long time to count. There's not necessarily anything nefarious about it. But every other state manages to --

CORNISH: Will it help if the president brings it up in his speech?

KILEY: I don't think it necessarily helps. I think that those of us -- there's a broad consensus that California needs to do a better job of counting votes. And, you know, I think that it just should focus on that as an issue of basic administrative competence.

CORNISH: Lastly, we started the show talking about this vote on military humanitarian aid to Israel. What was your vote? And give us your reason.

KILEY: I voted against the amendment to cut off the aid. Maintaining the American-Israeli relationship, which is mutually beneficial, which is vital to our national security interests in America, has always been broadly bipartisan. It was bipartisan yesterday as well. Hakeem Jeffries, the Democrat leader, opposed that amendment, too. So, I was glad to see that it failed overwhelmingly. Though it is concerning that you had 100 some members who voted for it.

CORNISH: We heard people like AOC. We also heard Jasmine Crockett make an argument that actually was turned towards the economy, turned towards the idea that the U.S. should not be spending this kind of money abroad.

And we talked about this at the start. And I remember Republicans said this kind of thing about Ukraine, right? Like, there's always a conversation in American politics about what it -- what foreign aid really means.

So, to you, what do you say to those voters who are questioning U.S. foreign aid to Israel and its war operations?

KILEY: Well, of course, every dollar needs to be heavily scrutinized. But if you look at the cost, if Israel were to lose its qualitative military advantage over Iran and its terrorist proxies in the region, these are groups, Hezbollah, Hamas, who do not like the United States and could pose a national security risk to the United States. And so having a strong alliance with the one democratic country in the region, having mutually beneficial technology sharing agreements, intelligence sharing, that is absolutely in the national security interest in the United States and the cost, economic and otherwise, if we were to lose that, would be absolutely immense.

CORNISH: So, when you see the Gallup polling showing those shifts across the American public in their support for this particular Israeli government, do you think, gee, I've got more convincing to do to convince all these Americans that actually this is important or is there something else going on?

KILEY: Well, support for this particular Israeli government is a different question. Of course, you know, people can have different opinions about the Netanyahu government and others that have been at the helm in Israel, just as we'll have different opinions of our own government here in the United States from administration to administration. But the American-Israeli alliance is something that has been enduring and has been bipartisan. And so --

CORNISH: But one affects the other.

KILEY: But certainly, one affects the other. But I think that we need to understand that this is something that is larger than any one administration in either country when it comes to America's national interests.

CORNISH: OK, Congressman, stick around. This is Congressman Kevin Kiley of California.

Next on CNN, Georgia's 2020 election results could be part of the president's address tonight. Former lieutenant Governor Geoff Duncan joins us.

Plus, there is a search for a leaker inside the West Wing. How far officials are going to go to find the source.

CORNISH: OK, so President Trump's primetime address tonight likely aiming to put the 2020 election back in focus. And that means putting Georgia back in the spotlight. Several investigations found the election was free and fair. The president still doesn't buy it. Six years later, people he backs for important roles are still backing the false narrative of a stolen election.

SEN. JON OSSOFF (D-GA): Who won the 2020 election?

JAY CLAYTON, DNI NOMINEE: You know, we're not -- I'm not -- I'm not going to do this with you.

OSSOFF: Who won the 2020 election?

CLAYTON: I think I've answered the question. We can keep doing this.

OSSOFF: You refuse to answer a basic question about who won a presidential election --

OSSOFF: But you asked to lead America's intelligence community? Isn't it humiliating to be unable to answer this question, to have to indulge the president's delusions?

CORNISH: Joining me now, Georgia's now former Republican lieutenant governor, who held office during that election, Geoff Duncan.

So, needless to say, you have had this conversation with Trump officials in the past. What did you make of how Blanche answered?

GEOFF DUNCAN FORMER GEORGIA LIEUTENANT GOVERNOR: Well, good for Jon Ossoff and the others that held him accountable in the public square.

I mean, look, this is -- this is so hard to believe that not only do people of this stature actually follow these narratives and try to push these conspiracy theories, but the others, the masses, follow them and believe them, either for political expediency or just because they just don't take the time to actually pay attention to the details.

I think what's going to be frustrating tonight is that if history predicts the future, then this speech is going to be an hour long worth of just conspiracy theories and a mile wide and an inch deep with no facts. And it's going to be talking about solutions in search of a problem over and over and over and over again.

CORNISH: I want to ask you about this. Jon Ossoff obviously leads the Senate race in Georgia right now according to a Fox News poll, 56 to 43. Now, Manu Raju of CNN was asking his Republican opponent, Mike Collins, a similar question, and here's how Collins answered.

REP. MIKE COLLINS (R), GEORGIA SENATE CANDIDATE: And that's what happened in 2020. They changed the rules up. You don't all of a sudden turn around and start mailing out absentee ballots to everybody. You don't set up drop boxes all over the place for people to just start this mass harvesting, ballot harvesting.

MANU RAJU, CNN CHIEF CONGRESSIONAL CORRESPONDENT: Do you think Trump won?

COLLINS: Trump won that race.

CORNISH: Georgia is an interesting place because people like you ended up kind of turning against Trump and the Republican Party itself. It's the state that gives us Warnock, but also Marjorie Taylor Greene. At this point, what is the sort of political fallout in Georgia from the persistent dialog about 2020?

DUNCAN: Well, it's answers like that from Mike Collins and the job that Jon Ossoff has done, which is the reason why Jon Ossoff is going to win this race. I think Georgians are smart enough to be able to pay attention to the actual person. I mean, I think that's why you saw, in previous election cycles, Raphael Warnock win and Brian Kemp win. One, obviously, a Democrat. One, obviously, a Republican. And so, I think Georgians are able to pay attention.

And we've seen this play out in three dimensions. We've watched Donald Trump come in and literally rip the soul out of a state just because he was too prideful to admit he got whipped. He got whipped because he was in a terrible place with the voters. He wanted to double down on nonsense. And then he caused two U.S. senators to lose because he put a ring in their nose and dragged them around with a chain for literally weeks in a runoff, trying to make them agree with the fact that the election was rigged.

It wasn't rigged. We all know that. Were there things that we can do to improve? We should all be open-minded enough to know that this is an iterative process with elections. We should make improvements as time goes on. But there was nothing that was rigged in that election we (ph) know.

CORNISH: And we expect to -- yes, we expect to hear the president also talk about the SAVE Act, which is something he's pushing for, which would involve new I.D. requirements for states.

So, I'm sure we're going to hear a lot more. Geoff Duncan, thank you so much.

Very quickly, Mike, I wanted to get your opinion, just because him saying like a ring in the nose, you know what I mean? Like, he -- this is a good example of how irritated some Republicans were in Georgia after the pressure to overturn the election. But him bringing in the fact that he thinks this affects, like Trump going into different races and trying to make a difference, he doesn't see it as a good thing. DUBKE: I think what we saw yesterday in the Clayton testimony with the

Democrats, one after the other after the other, asking the same question, that was -- that was performance art by the Democrats, asking somebody who has been nominated for a job to say something that his -- the new boss doesn't believe.

Whether or not you agree with Donald Trump. Donald Trump does believe he won in 2020. And this -- I am so tired of this question being asked because I do think it is performative art by the -- by the Democrats and the fact that Republicans keep bringing it up.

CORNISH: OK, Meghan, jump in.

DUBKE: I'm sorry, I just --

CORNISH: I wanted to get your point of --

DUBKE: It drives me nuts. They don't get to real issues because they keep wanting to ask this question.

HAYS: But here's the thing. But if they -- it's an integrity --

CORNISH: They don't want to say two and two is five.

HAYS: It's an integrity question and it's who you stand for. and you're going to debate someone, Jay Clayton, who is very well respected, is going to debase himself knowing Joe Biden won that election --

DUBKE: Clayton yesterday said that Joe Biden won the electoral votes.

HAYS: Then why couldn't he just say yes to Jon Ossoff?

DUBKE: He did. He did say that.

HAYS: When Jon Ossoff asked, why didn't he say yes? Because that's core stuff.

DUBKE: Because he's not going to play the game with the Democrats. He --

HAYS: And he -- now he humiliated himself.

sentences.analysis: 这是一个并列复合句，第一个分句主谓宾，第二个分句主谓宾，宾语从句由that引导，从句中主语this，系动词is，表语litmus test，for whether...or...作定语。

sentences.en: And then there are the Democrats who are middle-class working people who are like, why are we spending money and can't afford things?
sentences.cn: 然后还有那些中产阶级工薪阶层的民主党人，他们觉得，为什么我们要花钱却买不起东西？
sentences.structure: there be句型，主语the Democrats，两个who引导定语从句。
sentences.analysis: there be结构，主语后接两个定语从句，第二个定语从句中like后接直接引语。

sentences.en: And you can see that in some of these races, that they are not voting for people based on their stance on Israel or Gaza, or whether they called it a genocide.
sentences.cn: 你可以看到，在一些竞选中，他们不是根据候选人对以色列或加沙的立场，或者是否称之为种族灭绝来投票。
sentences.structure: 主谓宾结构，主语you，谓语can see，宾语从句由that引导。
sentences.analysis: 主句主谓宾，宾语从句中主语they，谓语are not voting，based on...作状语。

sentences.en: And I do think there is something specific to Israel because of the horrific nature of the crimes in Gaza, voters understand that this is an accurate litmus test for whether or not a politician shares their morality and whether or not a politician is beholden to their donors over their voters.
sentences.cn: 我确实认为以色列有特殊性，因为加沙罪行的可怕性质，选民明白这是检验政客是否与他们有相同道德观、是否更受捐助者而非选民影响的准确试金石。

sentences.en: This is part of the -- kind of the long-term gain, short term pain that I've talked about, that the Trump administration has created for itself by running primary opponents in Texas and Louisiana and elsewhere.
sentences.cn: 这是我之前谈到的长期收益、短期痛苦的一部分，特朗普政府通过在德克萨斯州、路易斯安那州等地支持初选对手而给自己制造了这种局面。
sentences.structure: 主句 + 同位语从句（that the Trump administration...）
sentences.analysis: 主句为This is part of the long-term gain, short term pain，后面that引导同位语从句解释说明这种gain and pain，从句中by running...作方式状语。

sentences.en: When you're at war with the judicial system, when the 16,000 people have quit the DOJ or been fired, when judges no longer can have, you know, trust in DOJ lawyers because there's been so many misleading and lies presented in cases, that's a real problem.
sentences.cn: 当你与司法系统为敌，当16000人从司法部辞职或被解雇，当法官不再信任司法部律师，因为在案件中有如此多的误导和谎言，那才是真正的问题。
sentences.structure: 三个when引导的时间状语从句并列，主句为that's a real problem
sentences.analysis: 三个when从句构成排比，强调问题的严重性，主句that指代前述情况。

sentences.en: But I think that we need to understand that this is something that is larger than any one administration in either country when it comes to America's national interests.
sentences.cn: 但我认为我们需要明白，就美国的国家利益而言，这比任何一国的任何一届政府都要重大。
sentences.structure: 主句+宾语从句（that we need to understand that...），其中第二个that引导表语从句，内含when it comes to...状语
sentences.analysis: 多层嵌套：think后接宾语从句，understand后接另一个宾语从句，其中something后接定语从句，when it comes to为固定短语作状语。

sentences.en: I think what's going to be frustrating tonight is that if history predicts the future, then this speech is going to be an hour long worth of just conspiracy theories and a mile wide and an inch deep with no facts.
sentences.cn: 我认为今晚令人沮丧的是，如果历史预示未来，那么这次演讲将是一个小时长的阴谋论，宽泛而肤浅，毫无事实依据。
sentences.analysis: 主句I think后接省略that的宾语从句，从句主语为what's going to be frustrating tonight，系动词is后接that引导的表语从句，表语从句中包含if条件句。

sentences.en: And we've seen this play out in three dimensions: we've watched Donald Trump come in and literally rip the soul out of a state just because he was too prideful to admit he got whipped.
sentences.cn: 我们已经从三个维度看到了这一点：我们看到唐纳德·特朗普介入并几乎掏空了一个州的灵魂，仅仅因为他过于骄傲而不愿承认自己被打败了。
sentences.analysis: 主句we've seen this play out，冒号后为同位语具体说明三个维度之一，其中just because引导原因状语从句，too...to结构表示“太……而不能”。

sentences.en: He wanted to double down on nonsense, and then he caused two U.S. senators to lose because he put a ring in their nose and dragged them around with a chain for literally weeks in a runoff, trying to make them agree with the fact that the election was rigged.
sentences.cn: 他想在胡说八道上加倍下注，然后他导致两位美国参议员落选，因为他在决选中给他们戴上鼻环，用链子牵着他们走了整整几周，试图让他们同意选举被操纵的事实。
sentences.structure: 并列句（He wanted... and then he caused...），后接because原因状语从句，其中trying to...为伴随状语
sentences.analysis: 主句为两个并列动作，because从句解释原因，从句中put和dragged并列，trying to make them agree为现在分词短语作伴随状语，that引导同位语从句修饰fact。

sentences.en: Whether or not you agree with Donald Trump, Donald Trump does believe he won in 2020, and this -- I am so tired of this question being asked because I do think it is performative art by the Democrats and the fact that Republicans keep bringing it up.
sentences.cn: 无论你是否同意唐纳德·特朗普，唐纳德·特朗普确实相信他在2020年赢了，而这个问题——我已经厌倦了被问这个问题，因为我确实认为这是民主党的表演艺术，而且共和党人不断提起它。
sentences.structure: 让步状语从句（Whether or not...）+主句（Donald Trump does believe...）+并列句（and this...）
sentences.analysis: Whether or not引导让步状语，主句为Donald Trump does believe后接省略that的宾语从句，and后为另一分句，其中because引导原因状语从句，从句中and连接两个原因。

sentences.en: But I will say, being in the White House in 2017 and having to deal with all of the Democrats saying that Donald Trump wasn't properly elected because he didn't win the popular vote in 2017, even though he won the electoral vote, which is the way we play this game in America -- that was -- we dealt with that for months, and it got played over and over and over again.
sentences.cn: 但我要说，2017年在白宫时，不得不应对所有民主党人说唐纳德·特朗普没有被合法选举，因为他没有赢得普选票，尽管他赢得了选举人票——这是我们在美国玩这个游戏的方式——我们处理了几个月，而且它被一遍又一遍地播放。
sentences.structure: 主句（I will say）+宾语从句（being...and having to deal with...），其中saying后接that宾语从句，从句中包含because原因状语和even though让步状语，which引导非限制性定语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句I will say后接省略that的宾语从句，从句主语为动名词短语being...and having to deal with，deal with后接all of the Democrats saying...，saying后接that宾语从句，从句中because和even though引导状语，which指代the electoral vote。

topics.title: 美国对以色列援助政策分歧
topics.content: 美国众议院就停止对以色列军事援助的修正案进行投票，103名民主党人支持，但修正案最终失败。这反映了美国两党内部对以色列政策的深刻分歧，以及选民态度向反对援助的转变。
topics.keywords: Israel aid, House vote, Democratic Party, progressive, AIPAC

topics.title: 美国公众对以色列看法变化
topics.content: 盖洛普民调显示，美国对以色列的净支持率在五年内下降29个百分点，年轻选民尤其不支持。这导致国会议员面临选民压力，重新考虑对以政策。
topics.keywords: Gallup poll, public opinion, Israel, favorability, young voters

topics.title: 美军睾酮筛查计划
topics.content: 国防部长皮特·赫格塞斯批准对30岁以上军人进行睾酮缺乏筛查，并允许自愿治疗。此举被视为文化战争的一部分，旨在塑造符合其理想形象的军队，但也引发对健康风险和军事部署影响的担忧。
topics.keywords: testosterone, military, Pete Hegseth, screening, culture war

topics.title: 特朗普政府泄密与爱泼斯坦文件争议
topics.content: 副总统J.D.万斯承认政府在爱泼斯坦文件沟通上犯错，代理司法部长托德·布兰奇在听证会上为此道歉。同时，特朗普政府内部追查泄密者，没收手机。
topics.keywords: Epstein files, J.D. Vance, Todd Blanche, leak, hearing

topics.title: 民主党内部意识形态斗争
topics.content: 以色列援助投票暴露了民主党内部进步派与温和派之间的“内战”。进步派主张停止援助，而温和派更关注国内经济问题，如可负担性危机。
topics.keywords: Democratic Party, civil war, progressive, moderate, foreign aid

topics.title: ICE执法与改革
topics.content: ICE（移民和海关执法局）近期因交通拦截导致多起致命事件，引发对执法方式、监督和改革的讨论。部分共和党议员也呼吁暂停或改革交通拦截做法，而特朗普政府则强调其有效性。
topics.keywords: ICE, traffic stops, body cameras, judicial warrants, reform, oversight

topics.title: 2020年大选争议
topics.content: 特朗普及其支持者持续质疑2020年大选结果，声称存在舞弊。尽管多次调查确认选举公正，但这一叙事仍在共和党内有影响力，影响提名人和选举政治。
topics.keywords: 2020 election, stolen election, false narrative, conspiracy theories, Georgia

topics.title: 美以关系与对外援助
topics.content: 美国对以色列的军事援助引发争议，部分民主党议员主张削减，但两党主流支持维持盟友关系，认为符合美国国家安全利益。
topics.keywords: Israel, military aid, bipartisan, national security, qualitative military advantage

topics.title: 加州选举计票缓慢
topics.content: 加州因计票速度慢受到批评，导致选举结果延迟公布，引发公众信任问题。两党均认为需要改革以提高效率。
topics.keywords: California, vote counting, delay, public trust, election integrity

topics.title: 司法部政治化
topics.content: 特朗普任命的代理司法部长在听证会上被质疑是否服务于总统个人而非公众，反映出司法部独立性的担忧。
topics.keywords: DOJ, politicization, independence, Todd Blanche, Trump's personal attorney
